coredata.dc:title: Aptamer-Based electrochemiluminescent detection of MCF-7 cancer cells based on carbon quantum dots coated mesoporous silica nanoparticles
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Su, Min
Liu, Heng
Ge, Lei
Wang, Yanhu
Ge, Shenguang
Yu, Jinghua
Yan, Mei
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we developed a novel electrochemiluminescence (ECL) platform for ultrasensitive and selective detection of MCF-7 cancer cells. To construct the platform, three-dimensional macroporous AuNPs@graphene complex was prepared through freeze drying process to modify glassy carbon electrode, which provided an effective matrix for concanavalin A to capture cancer cells due to its high surface area-to-weight ratio and excellent mechanical properties. The carbon quantum dots (CQDs) coated mesoporous silica nanoparticles were used as excellent ECL tracers due to their low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility. Then, the prepared tracers were conjugated with mucin1 aptamer to specifically bind mucin1 on cancer cells with high stability and bioactivity. Structure characterization was obtained by means of transmission electron microscopy and scanning electron microscopy images. The proposed method showed a good analytical performance for the detection of MCF-7 cancer cells ranging from 500 to 2×107 cells·mL−1 with a detection limit of 230 cellsmL−1. The as-proposed device has the advantages of high sensitivity, nice specificity, and good stability and could offer great promise for sensitive detection of MCF-7 cancer cells in response to therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence
CQDs coated mesoporous silica nanoparticles
Three-dimensional macroporous AuNPs@graphene.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: ECL detection of MCF-7 cancer cells with the cyto-sensor
Characterization of CQDs, MSNs, and CQDs@MSNs
Reproducibility, stability and specificity of the cyto-sensor
Possible mechanism for the ECL behavior of the system

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The fabrication process of the ECL cyto-sensor.
TEM image of (A) graphene sheets; SEM image of (B) 3D-GR@AuNPs; (C) TEM image and (D) size distribution of CQDs; TEM image of (E) MSNs and (F) CQDs@MSNs composite.

(A) PL spectra of (a) CQDs, (b) CQDs@MSNs nanocomposite; (B) EIS of the electrode under different condition in 5mM [KFe(CN)6]3-/4− solution containing 0.1M KCl: (a) bare GCE, (b) GCE/3D-GR@AuNPs, (c) GCE/3D-GR@AuNPs/Con-A, (d) GCE/3D-GR@AuNPs/Con-A/cell; (C) ECL-potential curves of (a) GCE/3D-GR@AuNPs/Con-A/cell/Apt-CQDs (ccell =1.2×106 cell·mL−1) and (b) GCE/3D-GR@AuNPs/Con-A/cell/Apt-CQDs@MSNs (ccell =1.2×106 cell·mL−1) in PBS containing 0.1M K2S2O8. Scan rate: 0.1 Vs-1; (D) ECL-time curve of GCE/3D-GR@AuNPs/Con-A/cell/Apt-CQDs@MSNs in PBS containing 0.1M K2S2O8 under cyclic scanning potential mode (-0.5V to -1.8V). Scan rate: 0.1 Vs-1. ccell: 1.5×104 cell·mL−1.

(A) Fluorescence imaging of the MCF-7 cancer cells after the incubation with CQDs@MSNs in the culture dish for 8h; (B) Fluorescence imaging of the MCF-7 cancer cells after cell capture onto the modified indium tin oxide electrode; (C) Effect of incubation time on ECL intensity; (D) Effect of the volume ratio of CQDs@MSNs (0.2gmL-1) to muc1 aptamers (0.2mgmL-1) on ECL intensity.

(A) Relationship between ECL intensity and cell concentration, each point was the average of ten measurements. Insert: logarithmic calibration curve for MCF-7 cancer cell. (B) Histograms for the specificity of the proposed ECL cyto-sensor. From left to right: 3.0×104 cell·mL−1 K562 cancer cells; 3.0×104 cell·mL−1 SKBr-3 cancer cells; 1.5×104 cell·mL−1 MCF-7 cancer cells.
Comparison of analytical properties of different immunoassys toward cancer cells.

head.ce:title: Aptamer-Based electrochemiluminescent detection of MCF-7 cancer cells based on carbon quantum dots coated mesoporous silica nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemical Sensing & Analysis in Universities of Shandong, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, (P.R. China)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ministry of Health Key Lab for Otolaryngology, Qilu Hospital of Shandong University, Jinan 250012, P. R. China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we developed a novel electrochemiluminescence (ECL) platform for ultrasensitive and selective detection of MCF-7 cancer cells. To construct the platform, three-dimensional macroporous AuNPs@graphene complex was prepared through freeze drying process to modify glassy carbon electrode, which provided an effective matrix for concanavalin A to capture cancer cells due to its high surface area-to-weight ratio and excellent mechanical properties. The carbon quantum dots (CQDs) coated mesoporous silica nanoparticles were used as excellent ECL tracers due to their low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility. Then, the prepared tracers were conjugated with mucin1 aptamer to specifically bind mucin1 on cancer cells with high stability and bioactivity. Structure characterization was obtained by means of transmission electron microscopy and scanning electron microscopy images. The proposed method showed a good analytical performance for the detection of MCF-7 cancer cells ranging from 500 to 2×107 cells·mL−1 with a detection limit of 230 cellsmL−1. The as-proposed device has the advantages of high sensitivity, nice specificity, and good stability and could offer great promise for sensitive detection of MCF-7 cancer cells in response to therapy.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is considered as the number one killer in many countries and has become a major public concern [1]. Breast cancer is the most common type of malignant tumor in women and it is the second leading cause of cancer deaths in women today [2]. Early diagnosis and timely treatment of cancer are the keys to improve patient survival rate at present. Thus, identification and detection of cancer cells is fundamental to therapy and a means of monitoring the relevant biological processes of cancers [3]. Recently, great efforts have been directed to develop novel immunoassays combined with fluorescence [4], electrochemical impedance spectroscopy [5], mass spectrometry [6] and electrochemical [7] methods to detect breast cancer cells. However, there are many limitations of their practical usages including poor sensitivity or selectivity. Compared with the traditional immunoassay, electrochemiluminescence (ECL) immunoassay, a chemiluminescence reaction initiated and controlled by the application of an electrochemical potential, is a promising technique in biochemical analysis due to its distinct advantages of simplicity, rapidity, sensitivity, controllability and low background [8].

However, the ECL immunoassay based on conventional luminescent reagents possesses some limitations. For instance, ruthenium labels may result in the loss of biological activity of the molecules, and ECL signal of luminol is weak in neutral solution, and the release of Cd2+ can result in a cytotoxic effect which limits the application of quantum dots (QDs) [9]. As a result, there is an urgent need to search for new environmental-friendly ECL reagents. Recently, similar applications of carbon based carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been reported by a few research groups [10]. The unique physiochemical properties of nanocarbon materials make them very promising in developing high-performance electrochemical and ECL immunosensors [11]. These investigations suggested that by using nanocarbons as the building blocks for immuosensors, it could not only sensitize the electrochemical response, but obviously improve the immobilized amount of biological recognition elements [12]. Recently, many efforts have been directed to mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSNs) for a wide arrange of applications such as drug release, sensors, catalyst and separation due to its unique structural features including a large surface area and high pore volume, ordered porous channels, a uniform and tunable pore structure, and a great diversity in surface functionalization [13]. In this work, mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSNs) were prepared to load more CQDs for signal amplification.

Aptamers, a kind of synthetic oligonucleotides discriminated by in vitro screening and systematic evolution of exponential enrichment technology (SELEX), are single-stranded nucleic acids and able to recognize and bind with specifically a variety of targets ranging from small molecules, proteins to whole cell [14]. Compared to antibodies, aptamers have better specificity due to their ability to fold into distinct secondary and tertiary structure [15]. Aptamers also have many other advantages over antibodies, such as low molecular weight, easy but reproducible production and low cost. In view of above advantages, aptamers-based assay potential alternative for well established antibody-based assay [16]. So far, various aptameric sensors based on electrochemistry [17], fluorescence [18], electrochemiluminescence [19] and other assays have been developed for cancer cell detection. Therefore, based on the discovery of DNA aptamers targeting mucin1 (MUC1), which is a glycoprotein expressed on the apical surface of epithelial cells and whose overexpression is often associated with some kinds of cancers, a novel ECL method for the detection of Michigan cancer foundation-7 (MCF-7) human breast cancer cells was proposed through the specificity recognition between the aptamer and MUC1 on the surface of MCF-7 cancer cells, as a proof-of-concept, in this work [20]. A new class of nanoprobes by integrating the functions of both specific recognition of MUC1 aptamers and signal amplification of CQDs@MSNs nanostructures has been developed in this work.

Graphene, a single layer of sp2 carbon atoms in a closely packed honeycomb two-dimensional lattice, has been directed to constructing biosensors due to its unusual electronic properties and large accessible surface area [21]. However, such surface expansion is still inherently limited by the two-dimensional nature. Therefore, in this work, three-dimensional graphene (3D-GR) was synthesized to attach more gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) on surface of the graphene skeleton, and improve the electronic transmission compared with two-dimensional graphene [22]. In this work, 3D-GR@AuNPs was prepared as biointerface for the capture of cancer cells. The electrode was first modified by the 3D-GR@AuNPs hybrid architecture, which can not only promote the interface electron transfer rate but also supply a number of AuNPs to stably accommodate multiple cell capture probes due to their good biological compatibility and large surface area [23]. Concanavalin A (Con-A, a lectin), one of the natural nonimmune proteins, can specifically recognize and bind to mannose [24], and it has a much higher affinity to oligomannose, especially the trimannoside Manα1−6(Manα1−3)Man, which is the core oligosaccharide of N-glycan on the cell surface. Furthermore, various glycoproteins on cell surface containing high mannose, hybrid, and biantennary complex types of N-glycan can be all bound by Con-A [25], thus enabling multiple binding between Con-A and mannose or trimannoside, which could improve the cell capture efficiency and thus enhance the sensitivity of this cyto-sensor [26]. Therefore, Con-A was employed as the cell capture probe to capture cancer cells in this work.

In summary, a novel ECL cyto-sensor for sensitive and specific diagnostics of cancer cells was developed employing the 3D-GR@AuNPs hybrid architecture as sensor platform and CQDs labeled MSNs as tracers. The proposed cyto-sensor exhibited an excellent precision and sensitivity in detection of MCF-7 cancer cells. Significantly, this new method may be quite promising, with potential broad applications in cancer early diagnosis due to the excellent analytical performance.

All reagents were of analytical-reagent grade or the highest purity available and directly used for the following experiments without further purification. The aqueous solutions unless indicated were prepared with ultrapure water. HAuCl4· 4H2O, polyethylene glycol (PEG), tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), trisodium citrate, (1-hexadecyl) trimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) and poly(vinylpyrrolidone) (PVP, K-30) were purchased from Sigma (St. Louis, MO, USA). Bovine serum albumin (BSA), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), and N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) were purchased from Alfa Aesar China Ltd. Phosphate buffer solution (PBS) (0.1M, pH 7.4) containing 0.1M K2S2O8 was used as the electrolyte in the measuring system. The ultrapure water was obtained from a Lichun water purification system (18MΏcm, Jinan, China). Calcein AM (CAS#C326) was purchased from dojindo china Ltd.

MCF-7 cell line was kindly provided from Shandong tumor research institute and maintained in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Cancer Medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% fetal calf serum (FBS), 1% penicillin/streptomycin and 1% nonessential amino-acids. Cells were cultured as monolayers and maintained at 37°C in a 5% CO2/95% air humidified atmosphere. The cells in exponential growth phase were collected and separated from the medium by centrifugation at 1000rpm for 5min and then washed thrice with a sterile pH 7.4 PBS. The sediment was resuspended in 10mM pH 7.4 PBS to obtain a homogeneous cell suspension. Cell number was determined on a Petroff–Hausser cell counter (U.S.A.).

The ECL experiments were carried out on a MPI-B multiple-parameter chemiluminescence analytical testing system equipped with a MPI-A/B multifunction chemiluminescence detector (Xi’An Remax Electronic Science & Technology Co. Ltd. Xi’an, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry Chinese Acamedy Sciences, China). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained from a JEOL JEM-1400 microscope (Japan). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained using a QUANTA FEG 250 thermal field emission scanning electron microscope (FEI Co., USA). Optic microscope images were obtained using a XDS-100C inverted microscope (caikon, china). The photoluminescence characterization was achieved on a LS-55 spectrofluorometer (P.E. USA). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out on an IM6×electrochemical station (Zahner, Germany). ITO glass (a thickness of ITO layer of 150nm and resistance of<15 Ώ/square; thickness of glass: 1.1mm) was obtained from Xiamen ITO Photoelectricity Industry (Xia-men, China). A conventional three electrode system was used for characterization measurements: a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) as the working electrode, a Pt and Ag/AgCl electrode as the counter electrode (CE) and reference electrode (RE), respectively.

In a typical preparation, graphene oxide (GO) was prepared from natural graphite by using a modified Hummers method [27]. To synthesize graphene dispersion, 10mL of GO (0.5mg mL−1) was diluted with 90mL of deionized water under sonication. After 5min, 50μL of hydrazine solution (35%) and 300μL of ammonia solution (25%) were added to the GO dispersion. Subsequently, the mixture was stirred at 90°C for 1h. After cooling to room temperature, the black homogeneous dispersion was separated by centrifugation and used for the following fabrication of composites. The detailed preparation steps of 3D composites are as follows: 5mL of GO (0.5mgmL−1), 150μL of HAuCl4·4H2O (1%, w/w) and 15μL of PEG were homogeneous mixing under ultrasonic for 1h, and then reacted in the oven under 180°C for 15h. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was separated by centrifugation and washed thoroughly with water. Finally, the 3D-GR@AuNPs composite was obtained through freeze drying process. The formation of a columnar ice phase and subsequent drying produced 3D porous graphene structures of a few microns in diameter and PEG was used as reactant here.

CQDs were prepared by using graphite rods (diameter about 0.5cm) as working electrode in an electrochemical cell in pH 7.0 PBS against a Pt mesh counter electrode (CE), an Ag/AgCl reference electrode (RE) applied potential at the graphite rod electrode was cycled between -3.0 and 3.0V at 0.1 Vs−1. The obtained CQDs solution was centrifuged (12,000rpm) for 20min to remove the non-fluorescent precipitate and the supernatant was dialyzed against water through a dialysis membrane (molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) of 1 kDa) to remove inorganic salt. Finally, the solution mentioned above were ultrafiltered through centrifugal filter devices with a 3 kDa MWCO membrane and CQDs were recovered [28].

MSNs with a diameter of about 200nm were synthesized according to previous report [29]. To synthesize MSNs, 0.3g of CTAB was initially dissolved in 120mL of deionied water, and then 1mL of sodium hydroxide (2.0M) was added into solution mentioned above under the condition of vigorously stirring in a water bath for 20min at 80°C. Later on, 1.5mL of TEOS was injected into above solution, and vigorously stirred for 2h until white precipitates were obtained. Finally, the obtained product was centrifuged and washed with deionized water and methanol, and then dried in air. Subsequently, 0.2g of the precipitates was refluxed for 10h in a mixture containing 1.8mL of HCl (37%) and 80mL of methanol to remove the surfactant template. The formed MSNs were separated by centrifugation, washed with deionied water and methanol, and dried for 6h at 60°C.

MSNs was first functionalized with PVP through mixing 2mL of MSNs dispersion (0.5mgmL-1) with 200μL of PVP aqueous solution (1.0%), followed by sonication for 30min to give a homogeneous suspension. After centrifugation under 10,000rpm, the complex was washed with water for at least three times and redispered in 2mL water with mild sonication. To conjugate with CQDs, the total of the above MSNs dispersion was mixed with 2mL of prepared CQDs solution and sonicated for 30min. After centrifugation, the colorless supernatant was discarded and the CQDs@MSNs composite was further washed with water for three times and resuspended in 2mL PBS (0.1M pH 7.4). Then, 500μL of the as-prepared CQDs@MSNs and 300μL of MUC1 aptamer (1mgmL−1) was mixed together. Subsequently, 100μL of freshly prepared EDC (20mgmL-1, in 0.1M pH 7.4 PBS) and 100μL of NHS (10mgmL-1, in 0.1M pH 7.4 PBS) were added. After being incubating for 50min at 25°C with gentle stirring, the mixture was disposed by centrifugation (12,000rpm, 15min). After removing the supernate, the precipitate was washed with water, and dispersed in the incubation buffer to 1mL aptamers solution. Finally, the resulting CQDs@MSNs conjugated aptamers (Apt-CQDs@MSNs) was stored at 4°C for later usage.

The schematic diagram of the sandwich-type cyto-sensor format was shown in Scheme 1 . The ECL cyto-sensor was prepared as following steps: Prior to surface modification, GCE (3mm diameter) was firstly polished with 1.0, 0.3 and 0.05μm alumina slurry respectively and rinsed thoroughly with absolute alcohol and distilled water in ultrasonic bath, and dried in air at room temperature. Then 5μL of 3D-GR@AuNPs (2mgmL−1) was cast on electrode and dried under infrared lamp. Subsequently, the electrode was incubated with 5μL of Con-A for 50min at 37°C. Next, they were rinsed with PBS to remove physically absorbed Con-A and blocked with 50μL 1% BSA solution containing 0.05% Tween for 50min at 37°C to block possible remaining active sites against nonspecific adsorption. Then the ECL biosensor was stored at 4°C in a dry environment prior to use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL detection of MCF-7 cancer cells with the cyto-sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the modified electrode was incubated with 5μL of cell suspension with different concentrations of MCF-7 cancer cells at 37°C in a humidified atmosphere containing 5% CO2 for 50min, then washed with PBS (10mM, pH 7.4) to remove any non-captured MCF-7 cancer cells. Subsequently, the electrode was incubated with 5μL of MUC1 aptamer coated CQDs@MSNs composites solution for 50min at 37°C and then washed thoroughly with PBS to remove non-specifically bounded MUC1 aptamer coated CQDs@MSNs composites.

Finally, the ECL signal of the cyto-sensor was triggered under the applied potential from -0.5 to -1.8V in 0.05M, pH 7.4 PBS with 0.1M K2S2O8 as coreactant (containing 0.5mM Ca2+ and 0.5mM Mn2+ for the remaining binding activity of Con-A) and the data was recorded by MPI-B multiple-parameter chemiluminescence analytical testing system.

The microstructures and morphology of graphene sheets and 3D-GR@AuNPs were elucidated by TEM and SEM as shown in Fig. 1 . The wrinkle and transparent graphene sheets can be seen in Fig. 1A, revealed the flexible of graphene due to its two-dimensional structure. As shown in Fig. 1B, the 3D-GR@AuNPs is a 3D porous structure with a smooth and thin graphene skeleton and the graphene skeleton is fully and uniformly covered by the AuNPs. Due to their good biological compatibility and electric conductivity, AuNPs were used to promote the electron transfer, which greatly enhanced the ECL intensity of the immunosensor. In this work, the composite were used as matrices for immobilization of Con-A and played a critical role in signal amplification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CQDs, MSNs, and CQDs@MSNs
ce:section.ce:para: TEM characterization of the CQDs (Fig. 1C) suggested that the as-prepared CQDs had a good dispersibility and a narrow distribution of diameters in the range of 1.5 - 2.5nm. Fig. 1E shows the TEM image of the synthesized MSNs. The TEM image of MSNs displayed a uniform diameter size of approximately 200nm. Significantly, it can be seen that many small pores were coated on or penetrated into the silica nanostructures. Moreover, the wormlike mesoporous structures with pore sizes of 2-3nm could be observed from the TEM images. Subsequently, by using positively charged PVP as a bridge, negatively charged CQDs could electrostatically attach onto the surface of the MSNs for the biofunctionalization of the MSNs. As shown in Fig. 1F, CQDs were coated on or penetrated through these small pores and the distribution was uniform and dense. Fig. 2A illustrated the photoluminescence of the as-prepared CQDs in solution under photon excitation of 330nm, and the maximum emission spectra (curve a) showed a peak at 490nm (blue fluorescence). The strong PL emission wavelength of the CQDs@MSNs nanostructure was at 500nm in the blue region (curve b), which could be attributed to intrinsic defects in MSNs as oxygen interstitials.

It is well known that EIS is an effective and convenient way to probe the changes of interfacial properties and to understand the chemical transformations and processes related to the conductive electrode surface [30]. Each reaction step of the modified electrode was analyzed by EIS in the frequency range 0.1-1.0 × 105 Hz, and in PBS (0.01M, pH 7.4) containing [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-(1:1, 5mM) as probe. Fig. 2B represents the Nyquist plots for the bare GCE, GCE/3D-GR@AuNPs, GCE/3D-GR@AuNPs/Con-A and GCE/3D-GR@AuNPs/Con-A/cell. A semicircle with a small diameter was observed at the surface of the bare GCE (curve a). After the modification of bare electrode with 3D-GR@AuNPs, a much lower resistance (R et) was obtained for the redox probe (curve b), implying that the 3D-GR@AuNPs was an excellent electric conducting material and accelerated the electron transfer. After the immobilization of Con-A, a remarkable increase in the electron-transfer R et value was observed (curve c), which suggested that the successfully immobilization of Con-A formed an additional barrier and further prevented the redox probe to the electrode surface. After MCF-7 cancer cells were captured onto the surface of the modified electrode, the R et further increased (curve d).

The ECL response of the as-prepared cyto-sensor (GCE/3D-GR@AuNPs/Con-A/cell/Apt-CQDs@MSNs) was shown in Fig. 2C. As a comparison, the ECL response of GCE/3D-GR@AuNPs/Con-A/cell/Apt-CQDs was also investigated under the same experimental conditions in each case. Results revealed that the ECL signal intensity of the Atp-CQDs@MSNs labeled cyto-sensor (Fig. 2Cb) was apparently 4-fold higher than that of the Atp-CQDs labeled cyto-sensor (Fig. 2Ca) at the same concentrations of cell (1.2×106 cell·mL−1). These results indicate that the analysis sensitivity was greatly enhanced using CQDs@MSNs as label, which was the result of a higher number of CQDs molecules loaded on the aptamers to achieve the signal amplification. Therefore, the CQDs@MSNs nanoparticles were promising for the construction of the ECL cyto-sensor. Furthermore, the ECL measurements of the Atp-CQDs@MSNs labeled cyto-sensor upon continuous cyclic scans in PBS showed constant signals after ten circles (Fig. 2D), indicating that the cyto-sensor we developed in this report showed excellent stability.

To inspect whether the prepared materials affect the cell viability, a standard staining method was adapted by the use of the calcein-AM (a living cell dyes). Low-toxic calcein-AM, which could be incorporated into living cells through the membrane, was chosen to carry out staining experiment for the determination of cell viability. Under the fluorescent microscope, green fluorescence could be observed in cytoplasm of MCF-7 cancer cells [31]. To perform these tests, MCF-7 cancer cells were incubated with CQDs@MSNs for 8h. Media in the dishes were discarded and the cells were gently rinsed twice with PBS. 3mL of the calcein-AM solution was added into the dishes and incubated for 30min at 37°C. It should be noted that, in Fig. 2E, CQDs@MSNs have no apparent effect on cancer cells. After captured by the 3D-GR@AuNPs/Con-A modified indium tin oxide electrode, MCF-7 cancer cells showed a good living morphology as shown in Fig. 2F. These results suggested that the materials as-prepared have no apparent harm to cells.

The goal of this study was to control the optimal experimental conditions to obtain the excellent performance of the proposed cyto-sensor for cancer cell detection. Several key parameters, such as incubation time and the ratio of CQDs@MSNs and MUC1 aptamers were optimized. The effect of incubation time of the MCF-7 cancer cells on the 3D-AuNPs@GR modified GCE was performed in the range from 10 to 70min. It can be seen that, with an increasing incubation time, the ECL response increased, and it started to level off and slightly increase after incubation 50min (Fig. 3C), indicating that the optimal incubation time of MCF-7 cancer cells was 50min. To obtain a high sensitivity, the ratio of CQDs@MSNs to MUC1 aptamers used to prepare the nanoprobes was optimized by investigating the change of the ECL intensity upon the increasing ratio (Fig. 3D). The ECL intensity increased with the increasing volume ratio of CQDs@MSNs (0.2gmL-1) to MUC1 aptamers (0.2mgmL-1) until 4:1, followed by a decrease, which was due to the fact that the aptamers on the CQDs@MSNs was insufficient for binding to MCF-7 cancer cells. Thus 4:1 of CQDs@MSNs to MUC1 aptamers was accepted as the optimal ratio for the preparation of the ECL nanoprobes.

To assess the sensitivity and the quantitative range of the proposed cyto-sensor, the analytical performance of the prepared cyto-sensor was explored by quantifying the cell solution with different concentrations including a blank one as a reference with CQDs@MSNs labeled MUC1-aptamer as tracers under optimized conditions. Fig. 4A showed typical ECL curves obtained in response to varying MCF-7 cancer cell concentrations. A linear relationship between the ECL intensity and logarithmic value of MCF-7 cancer cells concentration could be found in the range of 500 to 2×107 cell·mL−1, with a correlation coefficient of 0.9987 (N=10). The regression equations were I ECL =-4093.9+2103.8 lg c cells (cells·mL−1). The detection limits for MCF-7 cancer cells was estimated to be 230 cellsmL−1 at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3, indicating an acceptable quantitative behavior. Compared with previous studies (Table 1 ), a wider linear range and lower detection limit were achieved for cancer cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility, stability and specificity of the cyto-sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Since reproducibility was a very important feature for an cyto-sensor, it was necessary to investigate this feature of the developed cyto-sensor. The reproducibility of the proposed cyto-sensor was investigated with intra-assay and inter-assay precision. Sensing interfaces prepared on the same and on different electrodes were used to detect cancer cells with the same concentration. Standard deviation of five dependent measurements was less than 4% for the same cyto-sensor, and less than 5% for different cyto-sensor indicating excellent electrode-to-electrode reproducibility of the fabrication protocol described above. Both intra-assay and inter-assay applications demonstrated acceptable reproducibility.

In fact, as much as 91.9% of the initial response was preserved after storage of the cyto-sensor at 4°C for 30 days. The results indicated that the cyto-sensor exhibited satisfactory stability. This could be ascribed to the stability of the label and the cyto-sensor.

To investigate the specificity of the proposed ECL cyto-sensor, SKBr-3 cancer cells and K562 cancer cells were studied with the same experimental procedures in this work (Fig. 4B). When SKBr-3 or K562 cancer cells (3.0×104 cell·mL−1) were incubated with the cyto-sensor, the ECL signal was almost the same as the background signal intensity, while the ECL signal of buffer with 1.5×104 cell·mL−1 MCF-7 cancer cells was tremendously higher than buffer without MCF-7 cancer cells. Hence, the proposed cyto-sensor revealed a good selectivity for MCF-7 cancer cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism for the ECL behavior of the system
ce:section.ce:para: ECL, involving a light emission process in a redox reaction of electrogenerated reactants, combines the electrochemical and luminescent techniques. It involves the production of reactive intermediates from stable precursors at the surface of an electrode. These intermediates then react under a variety of conditions to form excited state that emit light.

For such ECL system of CQDs, the possible mechanism could be inferred according to previous reported literatures [36]. Obviously, the ECL behaviors of both CQDs are similar to that of other QDs, such as CdTe [37]. Consequently, the ECL mechanism of the CQDs was proposed as follows: Firstly, strongly oxidizing SO4 •− radicals and CQD•− radicals were produced by electrochemical reduction of S2O8 2− and CQDs, respectively. Then, SO4•− radicals could react with CQDs−• via electron-transfer annihilation, producing an excited state (CQDs*) that finally emitted light. The possible ECL mechanisms are described with the following equations:
CQDs+e−→ CQDs−•
S2O8 2− +e−→ SO4 2− +SO4 •−
CQDs−• +SO4 −• →CQDs* +SO4 2−
CQDs*→CQDs+hv

In this work, a novel ECL cyto-sensor for highly sensitive detection of MCF-7 cancer cells was developed employing MUC1 aptamers as a molecular reorganization element and CQDs@MSNs nanostructures as ECL signal component. In addition, MSNs with porous structure and large surface areas could improve the electron communication and provide more active sites for the immobilization of large amounts of CQDs. Moreover, 3D-GR@AuNPs, as an efficient bio-interface film, possessed excellent biocompatibility, enhanced the conductivity, and enlarged the specific surface area, was used to load a large amount of Con-A for the specific capture of cells and enhance the sensitivity of the cyto-sensor. Above all, materials involved in our experiments have no apparent influence on the cell viability. The proposed cyto-sensor shows excellent sensitivity and selectivity, wide linear range, good reproducibility, acceptable precision, and accuracy. In view of these advantages, this cyto-sensor has great potential in the field of disease diagnostics and clinical analysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Natural Science Research Foundation of China (21277058, 21175058, 21207048), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China (ZR2012BZ002).
